---
title: "Relapse Drift: Reading and Self-Assessment"
format: html
---
# Relapse Drift: Reading and Self-Assessment

Relapse is usually a process, not a single event. Many people notice a gradual "drift" first: less structure, fewer recovery contacts, more secrecy, more risky environments, and more permission-giving thoughts. Catching drift early is one of the most effective prevention skills.

A useful mindset is: **small changes matter**. Protective habits (sleep routine, support meetings, planned activities, nutrition, exercise, and honest check-ins) are not optional extras; they are core tools for staying alcohol-free.

Relapse drift usually unfolds across multiple domains at once: thinking, routine, emotional regulation, and social support. Tracking these domains helps you intervene at the earliest point, when course correction is still relatively easy.

## Read and Reflect
Consider each statement and mark **Often / Sometimes / Rarely**:

1. I have reduced recovery-supportive activities in the last two weeks.
2. I have been "testing myself" in risky settings.
3. I am minimizing warning signs instead of responding to them.
4. I feel less accountable to people who support my recovery.

::: {.callout-caution}
### When risk is higher

If urges or stress rise quickly, use this sequence early rather than waiting.

Choose one warning sign and one corrective step to apply today.

Early action usually prevents the cycle from becoming harder to interrupt.
:::
